name: guaraná
mechanism: Likely mechanisms are primarily methylxanthine-mediated (especially caffeine) adenosine receptor antagonism and CNS stimulation, with possible contribution from seed polyphenols to antioxidant-related effects. Clinical relevance beyond stimulant effects is still uncertain.

contraindications: Avoid during pregnancy and breastfeeding unless supervised by a qualified clinician.
Do not use with known allergy to this plant or related species.
safetyNotes: Safety concerns are largely caffeine-related: insomnia, palpitations, anxiety, and GI upset at higher total stimulant intake. Use caution in people with arrhythmias, uncontrolled hypertension, pregnancy, or high concurrent caffeine exposure.

sources.title: Paullinia cupana Kunth | Plants of the World Online | Kew Science
Guarana: revisiting a highly caffeinated plant from the Amazon - PubMed
Effect of Guarana (Paullinia cupana) on Cognitive Performance: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis - PubMed

description: Guarana is prepared from roasted, dried seeds of Paullinia cupana and is used in beverages and supplements. Reviews describe caffeine-rich seeds (often with additional polyphenols such as catechins) and longstanding use in the Amazon region.
dosage: Clinical studies use heterogeneous extracts and doses, so no single evidence-based standard dose is established. Product labels vary widely; caffeine-equivalent intake should be monitored.

Used in ritual, dreaming or folk medicine
interactions: Additive stimulant effects with caffeine-containing products.
Potential pharmacodynamic interaction with other sympathomimetic/stimulant agents.
preparation: Typically used as roasted seed powder, aqueous extracts, capsules, or incorporated into beverages/energy products.
region: Native to the Amazon basin in northern South America; cultivated and commercialized mainly in Brazil.
therapeuticUses: Traditional supportive use; indication depends on species part and preparation.
Evidence level varies; use only within established clinical or traditional context.
traditionalUse: Used in ritual, dreaming or folk medicine
sideEffects: May cause gastrointestinal upset (nausea, cramping, or diarrhea).
Possible headache, dizziness, or allergic skin reaction in sensitive users.
summary: Guarana (Paullinia cupana) is an Amazonian seed product with high caffeine content and traditional stimulant use. Human evidence is strongest for short-term alertness/fatigue outcomes; broader therapeutic claims remain preliminary.
